mcqs.question: In the Many-to-One multithreading model, how many kernel threads are used?

options.text: Zero kernel threads (entirely in user space)
Multiple kernel threads equal to user threads

mcqs.explanation: The Many-to-One model maps multiple user-level threads to a single kernel thread, so only one kernel thread is used for all user threads.

mcqs.question: Which multithreading model provides the highest degree of concurrency?
mcqs.explanation: One-to-One model provides true parallelism as each user thread has its own kernel thread, allowing multiple threads to run simultaneously on multiple processors.

mcqs.question: What happens when a thread makes a blocking system call in the Many-to-One model?

options.text: The thread library creates a new thread

mcqs.explanation: Since all user threads map to a single kernel thread, if any thread blocks on a system call, the entire process is blocked.

mcqs.question: Which model is used by the Java Virtual Machine on modern operating systems?
mcqs.explanation: Modern JVM uses One-to-One model where Java threads map directly to native OS threads for true parallelism.

mcqs.question: What is the main advantage of thread pooling?

options.text: Threads never block in a thread pool
Reduces thread creation overhead by reusing threads
Guarantees all threads run in parallel

mcqs.explanation: Thread pooling reuses existing threads for multiple tasks, avoiding the overhead of creating and destroying threads for each task.

mcqs.question: In the Many-to-Many model, what is the relationship between user and kernel threads?

options.text: Many user threads to many kernel threads
Many user threads to one kernel thread
User threads are not mapped to kernel threads

mcqs.explanation: The Many-to-Many model multiplexes many user threads to an equal or lesser number of kernel threads, allowing flexible resource allocation.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a drawback of the One-to-One model?

options.text: Limited by number of kernel threads the OS can support

mcqs.explanation: The One-to-One model creates a kernel thread for each user thread, and the number of kernel threads is limited by OS resources.

mcqs.question: What is a green thread?

options.text: A thread managed by the JVM at user level
A thread pool with only one thread

mcqs.explanation: Green threads are user-level threads simulated by the runtime environment, not by the OS kernel.

mcqs.question: In the Two-Level model, what is unique about some threads?

options.text: Some threads run in user space only
Some user threads are bound to dedicated kernel threads
Some kernel threads run without user threads
Threads can run on two processors simultaneously

mcqs.explanation: Two-Level model allows some user threads to be bound to dedicated kernel threads while others are multiplexed.

mcqs.question: Which scheduling is faster - user-level or kernel-level thread scheduling?

options.text: User-level is faster (no kernel mode switch)

mcqs.explanation: User-level thread scheduling is faster because it doesn't require a context switch to kernel mode.

mcqs.question: What is the primary limitation of the Many-to-One model on multiprocessor systems?

options.text: Only one thread can run at a time despite multiple CPUs

mcqs.explanation: Since all user threads map to one kernel thread, only one can execute at a time, leaving other CPUs idle.

mcqs.question: What is the thread library responsible for in user-level threading?

options.text: Scheduling and managing threads in user space

mcqs.explanation: In user-level threading, the thread library handles all thread management including creation, scheduling, and synchronization.

mcqs.question: Which multithreading model was used in early versions of Solaris?
mcqs.explanation: Solaris used a Many-to-Many model with Lightweight Processes (LWPs) as kernel threads, allowing dynamic adjustment.

mcqs.question: What is an LWP (Lightweight Process)?

options.text: A user thread with minimal resources
A kernel-level thread that can support multiple user threads
A special type of process that cannot create threads

mcqs.explanation: LWP is a kernel-scheduled entity that acts as a virtual processor for user threads in the Many-to-Many model.

mcqs.question: What is the relationship between concurrency and parallelism?

options.text: Concurrency is when threads overlap in time, parallelism is simultaneous execution
Parallelism requires multiple processes, not threads

mcqs.explanation: Concurrency means making progress over overlapping time periods. Parallelism means literally executing simultaneously on multiple processors.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of multithreading?
mcqs.explanation: Multithreading enables concurrent (not sequential) execution. Sequential execution would be single-threaded.

mcqs.question: What happens when user threads are bound to kernel threads?
mcqs.back: The user thread is permanently assigned to a specific kernel thread and cannot be preempted by other user threads.

options.text: The user thread is permanently assigned to a specific kernel thread and cannot be preempted by other user threads
The binding is temporary and can change
Bound threads run slower than unbound threads

mcqs.explanation: In Two-Level model, binding a user thread to a kernel thread ensures dedicated execution without being multiplexed.

mcqs.question: Which Windows threading model is used?
mcqs.explanation: Windows uses the One-to-One model where each thread in a Windows application corresponds to a kernel thread.

mcqs.question: What is the main goal of thread pooling?

options.text: To reduce overhead of thread creation/destruction
To maximize the number of concurrent threads

mcqs.explanation: Thread pooling reduces overhead by reusing threads for multiple tasks rather than creating new threads each time.

mcqs.question: Which thread library provides POSIX standard threading?
mcqs.explanation: Pthreads is the POSIX standard thread library providing portable multithreading for Unix/Linux systems.
